text: The surgeon closes the anal orifice to prevent the spread of tumor cells. The inner ring of the wound retractor is inserted into the anus, followed by the attachment of the GelPoint Mini single port device.
The surgeon closes the anal orifice to prevent tumor cell spread, inserts the inner ring of the wound retractor into the anus, and connects the GelPoint Mini single port device.

Three ports, including a camera port, are visible in use for the procedure. Carbon dioxide is insufflated into the surgical field at a pressure of ten millimeters of mercury, facilitating visualization and manipulation.
Three ports, including a camera port, are utilized for the procedure, with carbon dioxide insufflated into the surgical field at a pressure of ten millimeters of mercury.
The video displays the orientation of the operative field in relation to the anus, with a visual indicator located at the left lower corner of the screen.
The video displays the orientation of the operative field in relation to the anus, with this information shown in the left lower corner of the screen.

The surgeon begins rectal dissection from the posterior side, using electric cautery to divide the fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum. The dissection follows a steep curve, requiring careful navigation to avoid entering the mesorectum, and continues until reaching the sacral promontory.

The surgeon begins rectal dissection from the posterior side, using electric cautery to divide the fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum. The dissection follows a steep curve, with care taken to avoid entering the mesorectum, continuing until reaching the sacral promontory.
The surgical field reveals the sacrum, median sacral vein, and internal iliac vein, clearly visualized during the procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the sacrum, median sacral vein, and internal iliac vein during the rectal cancer procedure, highlighting these anatomical structures within the surgical field.

The surgeon extends the dissection to the lateral side, identifying the pelvic splanchnic nerve on the right side at the seven o'clock position and on the left side at the five o'clock position. Care is taken to avoid deep dissection behind the pelvic autonomic nerves.

The surgeon extends the dissection to the lateral side, identifying the pelvic splanchnic nerve on the right side at the seven o'clock position and on the left side at the five o'clock position. Care is taken to avoid deep dissection behind the pelvic autonomic nerves.

The surgeon extends the dissection bilaterally, revealing a wide exposure of the posterior surface of the mesorectum. Care is taken to avoid dissecting behind the hypogastric nerve, which is visibly lifted upward.

The surgeon extends the dissection bilaterally, exposing the posterior surface of the mesorectum. The hypogastric nerve is visible and should be avoided during dissection as it tends to be lifted upward.
The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, advancing through the intersphincteric space toward the head side. The puborectalis muscle is visibly separated from the mesorectum.
The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, advancing through the intersphincteric space toward the head side. The puborectalis muscle is carefully separated from the mesorectum.

The surgeon uses their left hand to push the mesorectum to the anterior medial side, revealing the dissection plane between the mesorectum and the pelvic splanchnic nerve. The pelvic autonomic nerves are carefully preserved during this maneuver.

The surgeon uses their left hand to push the mesorectum towards the anterior medial side, revealing the dissection plane between the mesorectum and the pelvic splanchnic nerve. The pelvic autonomic nerves are visible, and care is taken to avoid injuring them during the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the mesorectum on the right side, mirroring the technique used on the left side. The dissection then progresses to the left anterior side, where the pelvic plexus is visually identified.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesorectum on the right side, mirroring the technique used on the left side. The dissection then proceeds to the left anterior side where the pelvic plexus is identified.

The surgeon extends the dissection between the mesorectum and pelvic autonomic nerves from the lateral to the anterior side. The posterior dissection aids in identifying the proper dissection line, while the anterior side presents challenges due to the perineal body. Bilateral neurovascular bundles are confirmed as key landmarks before proceeding with anterior dissection.

The surgeon extends the dissection between the mesorectum and pelvic autonomic nerves from the lateral to the anterior side. The posterior dissection aids in identifying the proper dissection line. On the anterior side, the perineal body obscures a clear dissection layer, necessitating confirmation of bilateral neurovascular bundles as landmarks before proceeding.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the anterior lateral side of the prostate, taking special care to avoid injuring the urethra. Once the dissection plane just behind the prostate is identified, the anterior dissection proceeds more smoothly.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the anterior lateral side of the prostate, taking special care to avoid injuring the urethra. Once the dissection plane just behind the prostate is identified, the anterior dissection proceeds more smoothly.

The surgeon divides the perineal body at the inferior border of the prostate, clearly identifying the bilateral neurovascular bundles. The anterior dissection is extended to the lateral side, with care taken to preserve the pelvic autonomic nerves.
The surgeon divides the perineal body at the inferior border of the prostate, with clear identification of the bilateral neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon extends the anterior dissection to the lateral side, carefully preserving the pelvic autonomic nerves. The dissection between the mesorectum and neurovascular bundle is extended bilaterally, progressing until reaching the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon extends the dissection bilaterally between the mesorectum and neurovascular bundle. The anterior dissection progresses until reaching the pouch of Douglas, where the peritoneum is opened, allowing entry into the abdominal cavity. The dissection is performed just behind the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon performs dissection just behind the seminal vesicles, opening the peritoneum of the pouch of Douglas to enter the abdominal cavity.
The laparoscopic view shows the operative field as the surgeon transitions to the abdominal approach, typically involving the ligation of the inferior vessels and mobilization of the left colon.
The laparoscopic view shows the operative field as the surgeon transitions to the abdominal approach, preparing to ligate the inferior vessels and mobilize the left colon.

The surgeon connects the posterior dissection plane from the abdominal approach to the previously dissected space from the transanal approach, completing rectal mobilization. The right and left sides of the dissection are shown.
The surgeon connects the posterior dissection plane from the abdominal approach to the previously dissected space from the transanal approach, visually merging the two dissection areas.
The surgeon completes rectal mobilization, showing the right and left sides of the operative field, with the rectum fully mobilized.
The surgeon extracts the resected specimen through the anus. Subsequently, a hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis is performed, connecting the ileum to the rectum.
The surgeon extracts the resected rectal specimen through the anus. Subsequently, a hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis is performed to reconnect the bowel ends.
